comments.text: I think I made a counterexample to this. A short counterexample to the current online formulation of Problem #122. Let\[ R(x):=\frac{\#\{n\in \mathbb{N}: n+f(n)\in (x,x+F(x))\}}{F(x)}. \]I am only addressing the current online version of problem#122 as written above. I am not claiming to have resolved the historical original problem behind Problem #122. Assume throughout that\[ f:\mathbb{N}\to [0,\infty). \]I claim that for every such nonnegative function \(f\), there exists an integer-valued function \(F:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}\) such that\[ \frac{f(n)}{F(n)}\to 0 \qquad (n\to\infty), \]but at the same time\[ R(x)<2 \qquad \text{for every }x\in\mathbb{N}. \]This shows that the current online formulation is false under any natural interpretation in which the displayed ratio is supposed to become arbitrarily large as \(x\) varies. Indeed, define\[ F(n):=1+\left\lceil n^2+f(n)^2\right\rceil \qquad (n\in\mathbb{N}). \]Then \(F(n)\in\mathbb{N}\) and\[ F(n)\ge 1+n^2+f(n)^2. \]Hence\[ 0\le \frac{f(n)}{F(n)}\le \frac{f(n)}{n^2+f(n)^2}. \]By the elementary inequality\[ n^2+f(n)^2\ge 2nf(n), \]we get\[ \frac{f(n)}{n^2+f(n)^2}\le \frac{1}{2n}. \]Therefore\[ 0\le \frac{f(n)}{F(n)}\le \frac{1}{2n}\to 0. \]Now fix \(x\in\mathbb{N}\), and let\[ A_x:=\{n\in\mathbb{N}: n+f(n)\in (x,x+F(x))\}. \]If \(n\in A_x\), then\[ n+f(n)<x+F(x). \]Since \(f(n)\ge 0\), it follows that\[ n\le n+f(n)<x+F(x). \]Thus every element of \(A_x\) is a natural number strictly smaller than \(x+F(x)\). Consequently,\[ \#A_x\le x+F(x). \]Dividing by \(F(x)\), we obtain\[ R(x)=\frac{\#A_x}{F(x)}\le 1+\frac{x}{F(x)}. \]But\[ F(x)\ge 1+x^2+f(x)^2\ge 1+x^2, \]so\[ R(x)\le 1+\frac{x}{x^2+1}<2. \]Hence\[ \frac{\#\{n\in \mathbb{N}: n+f(n)\in (x,x+F(x))\}}{F(x)}<2 \qquad \text{for all }x\in\mathbb{N}. \]

I agree that the problem is incorrectly stated, as we could simply take $F(x)$ sufficiently fast growing, say $F(x) = x$. So my guess is that we should actually require $F(n)/f(n)$ to go to $0$ for almost all $n$, instead of the other way around. If that's not the fix, then surely we need at least some growth condition on $F$. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Yes, I think this is the correct interpretation: in [Er97] and [Er97e] there was just a typo (reproduced here) writing $f/F$ where they meant $F/f$. (Although this is more than just a typo in [Er97], since he explicitly states that the width of the interval (here denoted $F(x)$) 'tends to infinity faster than does [the normal order of $f$]'. Further evidence is looking at what [EPS97] actually prove - they don't state it as a main result, but their methods prove (implicitly) that there is an interval $I\subset [1,O(x)]$ of width\[\asymp \left(\frac{\log x}{\log\log x}\right)^{1/2}\]such that if $n\in I$ then $n+\omega(n)$ is in $J$, where $J$ is an interval of width $O((\log\log x)^{1/2})$ (and the normal order of $\omega$ is $\log\log x$). The motivation for this sort of problem is to generalise results like 'there are infinitely many $m$ such that $n+\omega(n)=m$ has many solutions'. In this formulation we replace $m$ with a short interval instead, and want many values of $n+\omega(n)$ in this interval. As is the case with many of the problems stated in [Er97] and [Er97e] there are definitely some errors here; also in the description of the results obtained (which don't really appear in [EPS97]). I'll email Pomerance to see if he can clarify and/or provide other references.
